info.topictitle: C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter

markdown:
# Transfer of Energy and Matter


## Energy Transfer


### Organisms


Most ecosystems rely on light energy from the sun.

Food chains show the flow of energy through trophic levels of a feeding relationship. A trophic level is the feeding position of an organism in a food chain. Food webs show all existing food chains in an ecosystem.

* **Autotrophs:** Make energy-containing organic nutrients from inorganic molecules by using basic energy sources. They use energy from light (photosynthesis) or inorganic chemicals (chemosynthesis) to form organic compounds with chemical energy.
* **Heterotrophs:** Obtain energy and nutrients by eating other sources of organic carbon molecules, mainly plant or animal matter.
* **Detritivores:** Energy is obtained by feeding on detritus and digesting internally, and they use chemical energy stored in organic molecules to fuel life processes.
* **Saprotrophs:** Obtain energy by secreting digestive enzymes onto detritus and then absorbing the products of digestion. They breakdown these organic molecules into inorganic elements (N and P).

### Processes


Energy stored in organic molecules (primarily carbohydrates and lipids), can be transferred via cell respiration to ATP. This is used in cells to support the metabolism (anabolism and catabolism, homeostasis, growth). Other inefficiencies include excretion of toxic molecules, egestion of not ingested materials, uneaten remains of an organism (bones, woody parts of plants). Energy is not recycled like nutrients are.

Only 10% of energy is transferred between trophic levels. The other 90% is lost by heat during cellular respiration and other metabolic reactions, uneaten biomass which contains chemical energy, and biomass that is not absorbed, such as that egested (excreted). Hence, no energy transfer is 100% efficient because of thermal energy, which cannot be converted into useful energy. Useful transfers from chemical energy are kinetic (muscular contractions), electrical (nerve impulses), light energy (bioluminescence), chemical energy (anabolism), sound energy (communication), and heat energy (body temperature



Energy flows from autotrophs to consumers via feeding, and is used up in respiration, growth, locomotion, and metabolism before being used as heat. Nutrients cycle as they are matter, and move between organisms and the physical environment. They are passed through the food chain and come back to soil, air, and water, meaning they pass through biotic and abiotic factors.

### Trophic Levels


Due to the waste of energy at every level of a food pyramid, there is a limit to how large this pyramid can grow. The organisms present are therefore divided into trophic levels, starting with autotrophs on the bottom, and following up through primary, secondary, and tertiary consumers.



## Carbon Cycling


The carbon cycle is a biogeochemical cycle whereby carbon is exchanged between the different spheres of the earth (atmosphere – air, lithosphere – ground, hydrosphere – water, biosphere – living things). Carbon is exchanged through various forms:

* Atmospheric gases: Carbon dioxide + methane
* Oceanic carbonates: Bicarbonates dissolved in water + calcium carbonates in corals and shells.
* Organic materials: Carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins in living things
* Non-living remains: Detritus + fossil fuels

Processes facilitate the cycling of carbon between forms (feeding, combustion). Some CO2 dissolves in water but most combines to become carbonic acid. Dissolved carbon dioxide and hydrogen ions are absorbed by aquatic plants, and other autotrophs that live in water. The hydrogen ions are responsible for the pH of water dropping.

Plants require a constant supply of carbon dioxide to photosynthesise. It enters through stomatal pores in leaves, diffuses down the gradient, an is used by the leaf. Organisms then carry out respiration to release energy as ATP. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of respiration.

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ca17012e656479b18e66896c3e78261b5aa5b097-850x681.png)

### Carbon Flux


Carbon fluxes describe the rate of exchange of carbon between the various carbon sinks / reservoirs. The main sinks are the four spheres. Carbon flux processes include:

* Photosynthesis: Removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and fixes it in producers as organic compounds
* Respiration: Releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere when organic compounds are digested in living organisms
* Decomposition: Releases carbon products into the air or sediment when organic matter is recycled after death of an organism
* Gaseous Dissolution: The exchange of carbon gases between the ocean and atmosphere
* Lithification: The compaction of carbon containing sediments into fossils and rocks within the Earth’s crust
* Combustion: Releases carbon gases when organic hydrocarbons (coal, oil, gas) are burned as a fuel source.

### Carbon Sinks


Partially decomposed organic matter is compressed into peat, which burns easily as a fuel and is a largely effective carbon sink. In soils, organic matter is digested by saprotrophs, which requires oxygen. Waterlogged soils are an anaerobic environment, so saprotrophs and methanogens are inhibited, so only partial decomposition occurs, which leads to the build up of large amounts of organic material.

Coal is formed when peat is buried under other sediments and heated over millions of year. Biomass can also be burned to produce carbon dioxide. Sea plants and animals die and are buried, and covered by layers of sand. Conditions are anaerobic so decomposition is partial. The remains get buried deeper over years, and so different oils and gases are formed (methane being the largest gas). Gas and oil are also partially decomposed ancient geological matter that has been compressed.

Methanogens produce methane from the byproducts of anaerobic digestion, mostly acetic acid and carbon dioxide. Methane can either accumulate under ground or in the atmosphere. When organic matter is burried in anoxic and acidic conditions, natural gas deposits can form. Increasing numbers of cattle can also contribute to increasing atmospheric levels of methane.

Methanogens are archean microorganisms found in anoxic environments (wetlands, digestive tracts of animals, marine and freshwater sediments, landfill sites). Methane releases only remain in the atmosphere for 12 years, before it is oxidised to form carbon dioxide and water. Hence, methane levels are not very high despite high emissions. Measurements show that atmospheric methane levels are increasing, although it can be removed by the oxidation of hydroxyl radicals.

![Image](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9ec0f172194b8185bb4eb5bf3dfd463ea68ad480-1600x925.jpg)
